(i).synopsis: Directors reward, long-serving employees, cradle to grave, employee expectation, company ethos, no State healthcare, statutory requirement, attract and retain staff
(i).question: List the reasons why a company would wish to provide a private medical expenses plan for its retirees.

Reasons for private medical expense plan: Such a plan could be used for directors to reward service (perhaps in a more tax efficient way than a salary or cash bonus). [½]
It could also be used to provide additional benefits to other long-serving former employees. [½]
It would ensure that staff are provided with 'cradle to grave' cover. [½]
This may be expected by employees based on past experience, ... [½]
... or it could meet the company's ethos around benefit provision. [½]
In particular, it may be expected if there is no State healthcare provided in the country. [½]
Alternatively it could be a statutory requirement for employers to provide. [½]
It should help the company attract and retain good quality staff. [½]

(ii).synopsis: Guarantees PMI for life, very expensive, claims increase with age, costs rise above inflation, complex to administer, tracking individuals, substantial liabilities, HR issues, spouse cover, longevity risk
(ii).question: Outline the disadvantages to a company of providing such a plan.

Disadvantages of providing private medic: This essentially guarantees PMI cover for life. [½]
This will be very expensive to provide given the likelihood of claims and amount of claims would both be expected to increase with age. [½]
Even without any additional risk, claims costs would be expected to increase each year at a rate greater than price inflation. [½]
This would be reflected in premiums for any available insurance product. [½]
If the company was self-insuring it would expect its costs to increase over time too. [½]
This would also be complex to administer. [½]
Individuals may move, particularly post-retirement, and keeping track of contact details may prove difficult. [½]
This will lead to additional costs and time for the company. [½]
The company would incur substantial liabilities that would need to be accounted for. [½]
Depending on the level of cover provided, it could lead to HR issues, for example if spouses were covered during employment but not in retirement. [½]
However, covering spouses in retirement would increase costs even further. [½]
If spouses were not covered in retirement there is a risk of accelerated claims before retirement. [½]
The company will be exposed to longevity risks. [½]

(i).synopsis: Hospital costs, in-patient, day-patient, out-patient, specialist fees, surgical fees, diagnostic tests, physiotherapy, psychiatric treatment, additional benefits, conditions including excess/limits/exclusions/acute only
(i).question: Outline a typical schedule of benefits provided under a group private medical insurance scheme for employees in a country with comprehensive free State healthcare such as the UK.

Core benefits: accommodation for parent accompanying a child [¼]
surgeons' and anaesthetists' fees for in-patient or day-care [¼]
diagnostic tests (radiology, pathology, X-ray, laboratory tests) [¼]
Additional benefits: cash alternatives if the State healthcare service is used [¼]
access to private out-of-hours GP and/or telephone helpline [¼]

(ii).synopsis: NHS waiting lists, waiting times for appointments and treatment, staff absenteeism, unavailable treatments, choice of hospital, MRSA risk, choice of timing, quality of care, overseas cover, cheaper than individual cover, attract and retain staff, competitive
(ii).question: Outline the points to be made in a letter to this director as to why this arrangement is provided for all employees.

Why PMI is provided for all employees: Although healthcare is free at the point of use to UK citizens there are waiting lists. [½]
There are waits for doctor and hospital appointments, tests and treatments. [½]
Waiting times for operations for debilitating but non-life threatening conditions can be significant. [½]
PMI reduces the overall medical treatment times and thus reduces staff absenteeism which reduces overall costs and/or increases productivity. [½]
Further, some advanced cancer and other treatments may not be available, ... [½]
... or they may not be available in all locations. [½]
Key attractions of PMI include: choice of hospital [½]
a perception of cleaner hospitals and reduced risk of infections such as MRSA [½]
a perceived better quality of accommodation [½]
a perceived better quality of care [½]
PMI can also provide cover when travelling overseas, [½]
Key attractions of employer cover: cheaper than individual cover [½]
attract and retain good quality staff [½]
remain competitive with others in the industry providing PMI to all staff [½]
